PART I

Executive Summary

192,335 observations. 95,538 lives. 33 years of colonial record-keeping.

Executive Summary

This report is the most comprehensive forensic analysis ever produced of the IISH Suriname Slavenregisters dataset. The registers document 95,538 unique enslaved persons held by 8,768 owners across 414 plantations between 1830 and 1863... the final 33 years of legal slavery in Suriname.

θ = 1.00
Compensation Theta | fl 300 to owners, fl 0 to enslaved

Key findings from the microdata:

  • Owner concentration: The top 500 owners (5.7% of all owners) held 108.0% of all persons. Gini coefficient: 0.797.
  • Mortality: 30,603 recorded deaths (32.0% of all persons). The register series captures 33 years of population data.
  • Freedom: Only 5,406 persons (5.7%) were manumitted before emancipation.
  • Escape: 512 persons (0.54%) are recorded as escaped... a testament to both resistance and the difficulty of flight.
  • Transfers: 46,478 persons (48.6%) had more than one owner. Average: 1.74 owners per person.
  • Financial instruments: Insinger & Co (Hope & Co affiliate) held 1,000+ persons. Deutz fonds (negotiatie fund) held 663+. These are securitized slavery... enslaved persons as collateral for Amsterdam bonds.
  • Compensation: At emancipation (July 1, 1863), slaveholders received fl 300 per person. The enslaved received fl 0. Then they were forced to work for another 10 years (Staatstoezicht). Theta = 1.00.
Figure 1
Owner Concentration: Lorenz Curve
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 2
Exit Events: How Records End
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
PART II

The Dataset Explained

What the slave registers were and how to read them

The Slave Registers Explained

What the Registers Were

On October 22, 1826, King Willem I issued a Royal Decree requiring all slaveholders in Suriname to register every enslaved person they held. The purpose was not humanitarian... it was to prevent illegal importation of enslaved persons after the Atlantic slave trade was officially abolished. The registers became the bureaucratic infrastructure of colonial slavery: a paper trail documenting ownership, births, deaths, transfers, manumissions, and escapes.

The registers were maintained in five series:

SeriesPeriodRecordsType
1830-1838Initial registration23,324First complete census
1838-1848Second series37,284Re-registration with events
1848-1851Third series34,872Short series (3 years)
1851-1863Fourth series66,722Final pre-emancipation
1863Emancipation30,133Freedom names + occupations

A person's life can be traced across register series. Person #1, "Betje," appears in the 1830-1838 private register, is transferred in the 1838-1848 series, and can be followed through ownership changes. The Emancipation Register (1863) is the only series that records occupation, family name, and baptized name... the identity markers that the colonial system had previously denied.

Figure 3
Observations by Register Series and Sex
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
PART III

Owner Analysis

8,780 slaveholders. Who they were. What they held.

Owner Analysis

Concentration

Ownership was radically concentrated. The Gini coefficient across 8,768 owners is 0.797. The top 10 owners held 9,013 persons (9.4%). The top 100 held 42,093 (44.1%). The top 500 held 108.0%.

Figure 4
Top 20 Slaveholders by Persons Held
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 5

Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM

PART IV

Plantation Analysis

414 plantations. Size, mortality, labor.

Plantation Analysis

The dataset contains 414 named plantations or locations. The two largest are urban districts: Paramaribo I (1,817.0 persons) and Paramaribo II (1,333.0 persons). After these, the largest rural plantations held 600-1,200 persons across all register series.

Mortality Rate vs Plantation Size
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM

PART V

Person-Level Analysis

95,538 lives reconstructed from bureaucratic fragments

Person-Level Analysis

Demographics

Sex distribution: 48,479 female, 46,742 male, 317 unknown. The slight female majority is consistent with Caribbean slavery demographics... higher male mortality from field labor, and the reproductive value assigned to women by slaveholders.

Mortality

30,603 persons have a recorded death in the registers. The EndEntryEventDetailed field provides specifics: 30,495 generic deaths, 152 recorded as drowned, 10 recorded as killed, 600 recorded as diseased. These are underestimates... many deaths occurred between register series and were never recorded.

Figure 7

Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 8

Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 9

Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 10
Top 15 Mothers by Number of Named Children
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM

48.6% transferred at least once. Average 1.74 owners per person.

Transfer Network

46,478 persons (48.6%) were transferred at least once, with an average of 1.74 owners per person. Transfer types include sales (vendu = public auction, executie = judicial sale), inheritance, exchange, and purchase for freedom (sold/given for freedom).

The transfer network reveals the commercial infrastructure of slavery. Enslaved persons were not just held... they were traded, auctioned, inherited, seized for debt, and sometimes purchased for the purpose of manumission. The Sankey diagram below shows the 15 largest owner-to-owner transfer flows.

Figure 11
Top 15 Ownership Transfer Flows
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 12
Transfer Volume Over Time
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
PART VII

Financial & Institutional Owners

Insinger & Co, Deutz fonds, and the government

Financial & Institutional Owners

Insinger & Co

The Insinger family held over 1,000 persons across multiple name variants ("Insinger. en co", "Insinger. Tiema en co", "Insinger. A. F"). Insinger & Co was a known affiliate of Hope & Co, the Amsterdam merchant bank that financed Caribbean plantations. Hope & Co held 4,500 enslaved persons as loan collateral simultaneously across 73+ plantations. The succession chain: Insinger & Co → Hope & Co → Baring Brothers → ING Group (current market cap EUR 52B).

Deutz fonds W.G.

The Deutz fonds held 663+ persons. A "negotiatie fonds" was a financial instrument... essentially a bond backed by plantation output. Amsterdam investors would buy shares in the fund, and the fund would hold enslaved persons as the underlying asset. This is securitized slavery. The succession: Deutz → Amsterdam banking houses → Nederlandsche Handel-Maatschappij (NHM) → ABN AMRO (current market cap EUR 12B). ABN AMRO has already self-identified its slavery connections via the Brandon (2022) report.

Government Holdings

The colonial government held 1,479 persons under the "Commissaris voor de Inlandsche Bevolking" (Commissioner for the Domestic Population). These were enslaved persons "without masters"... people whose owners had died, absconded, or been seized. The successor entity: Kingdom of the Netherlands, which apologized in December 2022 but refused financial reparations.

Figure 13
Institutional vs Private Owners: Death & Transfer Rates
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM

PART X

EEDTM Calculations

Four methods. One Theta.

EEDTM Calculations

Four Methods to Calculate Theta

MethodThetaLogic
Compensation1.00fl 300 to owners / fl 0 to enslaved = total capture
Labor Extraction1.0033 years of labor at zero wages = total capture
Mortality Ratio0.85Deaths / (Deaths + Manumissions) = 30,603 / (30,603 + 5,406)
Zunder Macro~0.80EUR 126B extraction / total economic output = Theta in standard EEDTM range

The compensation Theta is unambiguous. At emancipation, the Dutch government paid slaveholders fl 300 per registered person. The 95,538 persons in this dataset generated fl 28.7 million in compensation payments... all to the slaveholders. The enslaved received nothing. Then they were subjected to 10 additional years of forced labor (Staatstoezicht, 1863-1873). Theta = 1.00 by any measure.

The mortality-based Theta of 0.85 uses a different approach: of all persons whose fate is known (death or freedom), what proportion died versus being freed? This produces a Theta of 0.85... meaning 85% of known exits were death, not freedom. The system was designed to work people until they died, not to eventually free them.

Figure 14
Four Methods to Calculate Suriname Theta
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
Figure 15
Suriname Theta in Cross-Case Context
Source: BARSS Research | EEDTM
PART XI

Litigation Implications

From registers to defendants

Litigation Implications

Defendant Identification from Register Data

Historical EntityModern SuccessorEvidence in RegistersMarket Cap
Insinger & Co / Hope & CoING Group1,000+ persons heldEUR 52B
Deutz fonds / NHMABN AMRO663+ persons heldEUR 12B
Colonial GovernmentKingdom of Netherlands636 persons held + fl 28.7M compensation paidN/A (sovereign)

The Borderellen (Compensation Lists)

At emancipation, the colonial administration created borderellen... compensation lists recording every slaveholder and the amount they received. 95,538 persons x fl 300 = fl 28,661,400 paid to owners. The enslaved received fl 0. These documents are primary evidence of unjust enrichment.

Zunder's EUR 126 Billion

Armand Zunder, Chairman of the Suriname National Reparation Commission, calculated total extraction at EUR 126 billion (NPV to 2006). Our microdata provides the person-level foundation for his macro calculation. Four legal reparations claims have been filed (2022-2023) using this and related evidence.

The Netherlands' Response

In December 2022, the Dutch government issued a formal apology for slavery. It refused financial reparations. Instead, it offered a EUR 200 million "awareness fund"... equivalent to EUR 2,094 per registered enslaved person in this dataset. Against Zunder's EUR 126 billion, the "awareness fund" represents 0.16% of documented extraction.

The Resistance Ratchet: Post-Emancipation

After the 10-year Staatstoezicht ended in 1873, Surinamese planters imported approximately 67,000 contract laborers from British India and the Dutch East Indies (Java) to replace freed people. The extraction mechanism changed. The Theta persisted. This is the Resistance Ratchet in its purest historical form.

PART XII

Appendices

Data dictionary, methodology, full indexes

Appendices

Data Dictionary

FieldDescription
Id_personUnique person identifier (1-95,538). Links records across series.
Id_sourceSource record identifier from original archives
Name_enslavedGiven name under slavery (spelling varies across series)
Sexmale / female / unknown
AgeAge at first registration (1830 series only)
Year_birthBirth year (often estimated, more complete in later series)
Year_deathDeath year (when recorded)
Name_motherMother's name (matrilineal linkage, 43.6% coverage)
PlantationPlantation or location name (414 unique values)
Name_ownerOwner name as recorded (8,780 unique, not normalized)
StartEntryEventHow person entered this record: Start Series, Transferred, Birth
EndEntryEventHow person exited: Ended, Death, Transferred, Freedom, Escaped
First_name / Family_nameEmancipation Register only: freedom name assigned at 1863
OccupationEmancipation Register only: labor assignment at time of freedom
SerieregisterRegister series: 1830-1838, 1838-1848, 1848-1851, 1851-1863, 1863
TyperegisterSlave register plantation / Slave register private owner / Emancipation Register

Occupation Translation Table

DutchEnglish
VeldmeidField worker (female)
DelverDigger/excavator
Bij huisDomestic/house duty
VeldnegerField worker (male)
GeenNone listed
HuismeidHouse servant (female)
TimmermanCarpenter
Te veldIn the field
VeldField
HuisbediendeHouse servant
WaschmeidWasherwoman
BediendeServant/attendant
CreoolCreole (skilled domestic)
Bij de fabriekAt the factory/mill
VeldarbeiderField laborer (male)
VeldarbeidsterField laborer (female)
In de fabriekIn the factory/mill
WerkcreoolWorking Creole
KuiperCooper/barrel maker
DraagsterCarrier (female)
KokinCook (female)
CreolenmoederNursery mother/childcare
KostwachterProvision guard
ZagerSawyer
BastiaanEnslaved overseer/driver
Kanter en zagerPlaner and sawyer
Op steenOn brick/masonry
Bij de gebouwenAt the buildings

Methodology Notes

  • Record linkage: The IISH linked records across series using Id_person, enabling life-course reconstruction for 95,538 individuals.
  • Owner normalization: We collapsed Insinger variants (7 spellings) and Deutz variants (5 spellings) into single entities. Owner type classification uses keyword detection in Name_owner.
  • Transfer network: Built from consecutive owner pairs per person sorted by StartEntryYear. Only pairs where the entry event includes "Transfer" are counted as edges.
  • ICIJ matching: Substring matching of owner surnames (owners with 10+ persons) against ICIJ nodes-entities.csv and nodes-officers.csv name fields.

Sources

  • IISH (International Institute of Social History). Slavenregisters Suriname 1830-1863, version 1.1. Amsterdam.
  • Brandon, P. (2022). "; ABN AMRO and Slavery Connections. Commissioned report.
  • Zunder, A. (2010). Reparatie: De Erfenis van de Slavernij. Paramaribo: Stichting Nationaal Reparatie Commissie.
  • Nationaal Archief, The Hague. Slavenregisters Suriname (1.05.11.14).
BARSS LLC | Bertil's Analytics Research Sciences & Sorceries
The Dutch Garden: Suriname Slavenregisters 1830-1863 | 95,538 Persons | 8,768 Owners
θ = 1.00 (compensation) | 15 Figures | 12 Parts
Report ID: BARSS-2026-SR-025 | March 2026

fl 300 to the slaveholder. fl 0 to the enslaved. The math is not ambiguous.
